ground_truth.title: Quick-Start Guide - Domain Storytelling
ground_truth.main_content:
Your First Domain Story: Matthew runs a small movie theater called Metropolis. His friend Anna is an app developer. Matthew asks Anna to develop an online ticketing app because customers don't want to buy tickets in person anymore. Anna realizes she needs to understand how the cinema operates today. They conduct a workshop where Anna draws on a whiteboard, starting with actors: a cashier and a moviegoer. They work through the process step by step, with Anna drawing icons and numbering activities. The story shows how a moviegoer buys a ticket at the box office. Matthew later remembers to mention that international movies need to indicate the language. After several domain stories, Anna gains understanding of terms like 'seating plan,' 'show,' and 'to mark seats.'

The Pictographic Language: Domain stories use building blocks consisting of icons, arrows, and text. Actors are persons, groups of people, or software systems with an active role. Work objects are documents, physical things, or digital objects that actors create and exchange. Activities are shown as arrows labeled with verbs. Sequence numbers order sentences since stories are told one after another. Annotations provide textual context about variations, assumptions, and domain terms. Groups cluster related parts of a story, such as repeated activities, optional activities, or organizational boundaries.

Activities connect actors and work objects to form sentences with syntax: subject-predicate-object. Every actor appears only once, but each activity has a separate work object. This allows work objects to change status as the story evolves.

Scenario-based Modeling: Most process modeling shows what can possibly happen using conditionals and variations. However, premature abstractions are often wrong. Domain Storytelling recommends first establishing understanding of typical cases—the '80% case' or 'happy path'—before discussing alternatives. Each alternative becomes its own domain story. Small variations are handled with annotations rather than separate stories.

Scope: Domain Storytelling adapts to different purposes. Granularity determines detail level: coarse-grained overview or fine-grained details. Time scope describes processes as they currently are (as-is) or how they will be (to-be). Domain purity includes or omits software systems. Pure stories exclude software; digitalized stories include it.

The Workshop Format: Domain Storytelling is a conversation technique, not just notation. Participants include domain experts, IT experts, and a moderator. The moderator records the story graphically while asking questions like 'What happens next?' and 'How do you determine what to do next?' The story must be visible to all participants. When finished, the moderator retells the story from the beginning to confirm accuracy and check for missing elements.

Tools: Simple whiteboards with sticky notes work well. The Whiteboard Kit speeds up recording with pre-made icon templates. Digital tools like Miro or diagrams.net offer alternatives. Egon.io is an open-source browser-based tool specifically designed for Domain Stories.
with: Domain stories visualize who (actor) does what (activity) with what (work objects) with whom (other actors).
The basic syntax is: subject - predicate - object.
Every actor appears only once in the domain story. But for every activity, we draw a separate work object (even if the same work object already exists in a previous sentence).
We recommend you start with modeling the default case—the '80% case'—and the 'happy path' first.
Domain Storytelling is a conversation technique.
